title: Soupe à l'Oignon
subtitle: French onion soup with caramelised onions, beef stock, and a Gruyère crouton
description: French onion soup is built almost entirely on patience: a large quantity of onions cooked very slowly in butter for forty-five minutes or more until they collapse into a sweet, dark, jammy mass. The stock goes in last, the soup simmers briefly, and each portion is topped with a crouton and grated Gruyère then grilled until bubbling and browned. The slow caramelisation of the onions is the entire recipe.
sourceNotes: Soupe à l'oignon is documented in French cookery from at least the eighteenth century. It appears in Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and was popularised in English-speaking countries by Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961). The Parisian version using beef stock and gratinéed with Gruyère is the most widely known.

recipeTools.notes: A wide, heavy-based saucepan or Dutch oven — the width is important for even caramelisation across all the onions.
Traditional French onion soup is served and gratinéed in individual oven-safe bowls or small cocottes. Alternatively, use a single oven-safe serving dish.

glossaryTerms.definition: The slow chemical breakdown of sugars in food under heat, producing complex, sweet, brown-coloured compounds. In onions, caramelisation takes 40–60 minutes over a low heat; anything faster produces steamed onions, not caramelised ones.
To brown the surface of a dish under a grill or in a very hot oven. In soupe à l'oignon, the cheese crouton is gratiné until bubbling and golden.

ingredients.notes: About 6 large — finely sliced. The quantity looks alarming; they reduce dramatically.
Optional — for a slightly thicker soup
Hot — use the best quality stock you have

steps: Melt the butter with the olive oil in a large, wide, heavy-based pan over a medium-low heat. Add all the sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Stir to coat in fat.

Cook the onions, stirring every 5–8 minutes, for 40–50 minutes until they are deep golden brown and reduced to about a quarter of their original volume. Do not rush this step — low and slow is essential. If they are catching and burning in patches, add a splash of water and scrape the pan.
If using flour, scatter it over the onions, stir for 1 minute, and cook out.

Pour in the white wine, increase the heat to medium, and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the hot beef stock, thyme, and bay leaf. Season with salt and pepper. Simmer for 15 minutes. Remove the herb sprigs.
Toast the bread slices until dry and golden.

Heat the grill to its highest setting. Ladle the soup into oven-safe bowls, float a crouton on top of each, and pile with grated Gruyère. Place the bowls under the grill for 3–5 minutes until the cheese bubbles and browns at the edges.
Serve immediately — the bowls will be very hot.

of the onions. Forty minutes at low heat is not optional — it is where all the flavour comes from. Onions cooked at medium-high heat for fifteen minutes are not caramelised; they are merely softened, and the soup will taste thin and sharp. Allow the time.

Melt {{butter-unsalted}} of butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large, wide pan over a medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} of finely sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Stir to coat. Cook for 40–50 minutes, stirring every 5–8 minutes, until deep golden and reduced to roughly a quarter of their starting volume. If any bits are catching on the base, add a splash of water and scrape.

Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine, increase the heat to medium, and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add {{beef-stock}} of hot beef stock, the thyme sprigs, and the bay leaf. Season. Simmer for 15 minutes. Remove the herbs.
